data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-01T01:40:27.439Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.content:
Your code **does not use `Thread.Abort`** (or `ThreadAbortException`, `ResetAbort`, or `Thread.Interrupt`). It only uses `Thread.Start`, `Thread.Join`, and `Thread.Sleep` — all of which work identically in .NET 8 and require **no migration**.

That said, the modern idiomatic replacement for manual thread management would be `Parallel.ForEach` or `Task.Run` with `await Task.WhenAll`, but that's a modernization choice, not a migration requirement.

**No Thread.Abort migration is needed here.**
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating .NET Framework code that uses Thread.Abort to .NET 8. Let me look at the code they provided - it actually doesn't use Thread.Abort at all. It uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep, which all work fine in modern .NET.

Let me check if the thread-abort-migration skill is relevant here. The skill description says:

"USE FOR: modernizing code that calls Thread.Abort, catching ThreadAbortException, replacing Thread.ResetAbort, replacing Thread.Interrupt for thread termination..."

"DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."


The code they've shown only uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep - none of the Thread.Abort related APIs that would actually require migration. So I should clarify that their code doesn't need any changes for this purpose.
